text: Loop engineering is replacing yourself as the person who prompts the agent by designing the system that does it instead.

evidence.quote: Loop engineering is replacing yourself as the person who prompts the agent.

text: A loop is a recursive goal where you define a purpose and the AI iterates until complete.

evidence.quote: A loop here can be thought of a recursive goal where you define a purpose and the AI iterates until complete.

text: A loop needs five things (automations, worktrees, skills, plugins/connectors, sub-agents) and one place to remember stuff (memory).

evidence.quote: A loop needs five things and then one place to remember stuff.

text: The memory is the sixth thing a loop needs, a persistent store outside the single conversation that holds what's done and what is next.

evidence.quote: Then the sixth thing, the memory.

text: The model forgets everything between runs so the memory has to be on disk and not in the context; the agent forgets, the repo doesn't.

evidence.quote: the model forgets everything between runs so the memory has to be on disk and not in the context.

text: Automations are what make a loop an actual loop and not just one run you did once; they fire on a schedule and do discovery and triage by themselves.

evidence.quote: Automations are what make a loop an actual loop and not just one run you did once.

text: The /goal primitive keeps working until a verifiable stopping condition holds, with a separate small model checking whether you are done so the agent that wrote the code isn't the one grading it.

evidence.quote: /goal keeps going until a condition you wrote is actually true, and after every turn a separate small model checks whether you are done, so the agent that wrote the code isnt the one grading it.

text: Worktrees prevent parallel agents from colliding by giving each agent a separate working directory on its own branch sharing the same repo history, so one agent's edits literally cannot touch the other one's checkout.

evidence.quote: Two agents writing the same file is the exact same headache as two engineers committing to the same lines and nobody talked to each other first.

issues.detail: 1 argument(s) not found in the quote/span — advisory only

text: Skills are how you stop re-explaining the same project context every session, written one time where the agent reads it every run; a skill is that intent written down on the outside.

evidence.quote: A skill is how you stop re-explaining the same project context every session like a goldfish.

text: Plugins bundle connectors and skills together so your teammate installs your setup in one go instead of rebuilding the whole thing from memory.

evidence.quote: plugins bundle connectors and skills together so your teammate installs your setup in one go instead of rebuilding the whole thing from memory.

text: Connectors, built on MCP, let the agent read your issue tracker, query a database, hit a staging API, drop a message in Slack.

evidence.quote: Connectors, which are built on MCP, let the agent read your issue tracker, query a database, hit a staging api, drop a message in Slack.

text: The most useful structural thing in a loop is splitting the one who writes from the one who checks, because the model that wrote the code is way too nice grading its own homework.

evidence.quote: The most useful structural thing in a loop, by far, is splitting the one who writes from the one who checks.

issues.detail: 1 argument(s) not found in the quote/span — advisory only

text: The loop changes the work but does not delete you from it, and three problems actually get sharper as the loop gets better: verification is still on you, your understanding still rots if you allow it, and the comfortable posture is probably the risky one.

evidence.quote: The loop changes the work, it does not delete you from it.

text: Verification is still on you; a loop running unattended is also a loop making mistakes unattended, and even then 'done' is a claim and not a proof.

evidence.quote: Verification is still on you.

text: Comprehension debt is the growing gap between what exists and what you actually understand, which a smooth loop just makes grow faster unless you read what the loop made.

evidence.quote: Your understanding still rots if you allow it.

issues.detail: 1 argument(s) not found in the quote/span — advisory only

text: Cognitive surrender is when the loop runs itself and you stop having an opinion and just take whatever it gives back; designing the loop is the cure when you do it with judgment and the accelerant when you do it to avoid thinking.

evidence.quote: I called that cognitive surrender.

text: Two people can build the exact same loop and get completely opposite results: one uses it to move faster on work they understand deeply, the other uses it to avoid understanding the work at all, and the loop doesn't know the difference.

evidence.quote: Two people can build the exact same loop and get completely opposite results.

text: Loop design is harder than prompt engineering because the leverage point moved, not because the work got easier.

evidence.quote: That's what makes loop design harder than prompt engineering, not easier.

text: Build the loop, but build it like someone who intends to stay the engineer, not just the person who presses go.

evidence.quote:
Build the loop.
But build it like someone who intends to stay the engineer, not just the person who presses go.

issues.detail: quote spans a window `p050.s001`..`p050.s002` around the ref

text: Set up your loops, but don't forget that prompting your agents directly is still effective; it's all about finding the right balance.

evidence.quote: go ahead and set up your loops, but don't forget that prompting your agents directly is still effective.

text: You absolutely have to be careful about token costs because usage patterns can vary wildly if you are token rich or poor.

evidence.quote: you absolutely have to be careful about token costs (usage patterns can vary wildly if you are token rich or poor).

text: Skills let intent stop costing you over and over; without skills the loop re-derives your whole project from zero every cycle, with skills it kind of compounds.

evidence.quote: Skills are also where intent stops costing you over and over.

text: The five building blocks of a loop (automations, worktrees, skills, plugins/connectors, sub-agents) and the memory exist in both Claude Code and Codex, with the same capabilities under different names.

evidence.quote: Both products have all five now.

evidence.quote: Thats comprehension debt

text: The thing that surprised me is this is not really a tool thing anymore.

evidence.quote: The thing that surprised me is this is not really a tool thing anymore.
